coredata.dc:title: Li-ion cells internal temperature estimation using medium-frequency measurements of impedance argument
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Bhoir, Shubham Sharad
Thenaisie, Guillaume
Brivio, Claudio
Paolone, Mario
coredata.dc:description: The thermal runaway of Lithium-ion batteries can result in severe accidents hindering their adoption and is, therefore, a cause for concern. Monitoring the temperature of the cells in a battery is one of the most effective methods to anticipate a thermal runaway. Since readings from a temperature sensor on the surface of a cell may lag behind its true internal temperature, they cannot be used to predict the advent of thermal runaway. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) can be used to provide insights into the internal properties of the cell. This work builds upon a previous study that proposed a method to use the phase of the impedance of the cell to estimate its internal temperature, where the Distribution of Relaxation Times (DRT) technique was used to infer the frequency at which this impedance measurement should be carried out. In this paper, a comprehensive experimental validation of the proposed methodology is performed on a set of NMC Lithium-ion cells, by placing a temperature sensor inside them and comparing the estimated and surface temperature under various electrical and thermal loads. Furthermore, an investigation on the influence of SoC on the estimated temperature is also performed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion batteries
Temperature estimation
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Distribution of relaxation times
Battery safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discussion on the use of the proposed temperature estimation to monitor thermal runaway

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Power and speed profiles for the WLTC profile.
OCV-capacity curve pre- and post-sensor insertion.
Nyquist plots pre- and post-sensor insertion.
Nyquist and Bode phase plot for the EIS of a cell at different temperatures.
Temperature-phase correlation for the three cells, post-sensor insertion.
Temperature-phase correlation at different SoCs for a cell.
Current, voltage and temperature evolution for the NL test.
Current, voltage and temperature evolution for the OL test.
Current, voltage and temperature evolution for the HY test.
Current, voltage and temperature evolution for the DL test.
Time constants where DRT peaks appear and their corresponding physical processes.
Temperatures at which EIS characterization of cells is performed.
RMSE and maximum error on the estimation and surface temperature for the validation tests.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work was partially funded by Innosuisse – Swiss Innovation Agency , under the Innovation project “FLAGSHIP PFFS-21-20”, Agreement number : 2155009968. CSEM thanks them for their support.

head.ce:title: Li-ion cells internal temperature estimation using medium-frequency measurements of impedance argument

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sustainable Energy Center, CSEM, Jaquet Droz 1, Neuchâtel, 2002, Nauchâtel, Switzerland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Sustainable Energy Center, CSEM, addressline=Jaquet Droz 1, city=Neuchâtel, postcode=2002, state=Nauchâtel, country=Switzerland

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Distributed Electrical Systems Laboratory, EPFL, Lausanne, Vaud, Switzerland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Distributed Electrical Systems Laboratory, EPFL, addressline=, city=Lausanne, postcode=, state=Vaud, country=Switzerland

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Sustainable Energy Center, CSEM, Jaquet Droz 1, Neuchâtel, 2002, Nauchâtel, Switzerland.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The thermal runaway of Lithium-ion batteries can result in severe accidents hindering their adoption and is, therefore, a cause for concern. Monitoring the temperature of the cells in a battery is one of the most effective methods to anticipate a thermal runaway. Since readings from a temperature sensor on the surface of a cell may lag behind its true internal temperature, they cannot be used to predict the advent of thermal runaway. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) can be used to provide insights into the internal properties of the cell. This work builds upon a previous study that proposed a method to use the phase of the impedance of the cell to estimate its internal temperature, where the Distribution of Relaxation Times (DRT) technique was used to infer the frequency at which this impedance measurement should be carried out. In this paper, a comprehensive experimental validation of the proposed methodology is performed on a set of NMC Lithium-ion cells, by placing a temperature sensor inside them and comparing the estimated and surface temperature under various electrical and thermal loads. Furthermore, an investigation on the influence of SoC on the estimated temperature is also performed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As batteries are used, they may undergo various kinds of abuses. An abuse refers to the usage of a battery under operating conditions outside those specified by their manufacturer. In the literature, such abuses are broadly classified into three types, namely, mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuses [1,2]. Mechanical abuses include any kind of mechanical deformation of the cell such as crushing, or penetration by a sharp object. Electrical abuses take the form of over-charging/over-discharging of the cell which have their roots in a BMS failure event. A short circuit (either internal or external) is also classified under electrical abuse. Finally, thermal abuse occurs when the cell is used beyond the temperature limits specified by the cell manufacturer.

Several battery fires have occurred due to these kinds of abuses [3]. For instance, in September 2023, a Tesla Model 3 caught fire due to debris from a truck puncturing its battery pack [4]. In the state of Gujarat, India, fifteen e-rickshaws were set ablaze due to a battery fire in December 2022 [5]. Battery fires have also been seen in installations of stationary storage systems. In September 2023, a battery fire occurred in stationary storage facility in California [6]. During the same period, multiple cases of battery fires from batteries connected to residential PV systems have also been reported in Germany and Austria [7]. An increase in the number of such accidents is inevitable as the number of batteries in use increases, unless the safety of these batteries can be enhanced.

To increase the safety of cells, one of the most effective methods is to constantly track their temperature. This may enable the prediction of whether a cell will undergo thermal runaway in short time. The methods for this purpose can be divided into two broad categories: direct and indirect measurements [8,9]. Direct measurements are those wherein temperature sensors such as a thermistors or thermocouples are used to measure the cell’s temperature. An example of such a study is Zhang et al. (2014) where thermocouples were used to map the distribution of temperature within the cells [10]. However, a battery pack can contain hundreds of cells, due to which it may be impractical to have temperature sensors on each cell [11]. Moreover, there may be a difference between the internal and external temperature of the cell, the amount of difference depending upon the cell’s form factor [12]. Finally, placing a sensor within the cell may not be practical since it may not be able to withstand the harsh environment inside the cell. That being stated, the use of fiber Bragg grating sensors are attracting the attention of various researchers in the community due to their chemical inertness and their ability to monitor the temperature at various points along a single fiber at the same time [13]. However, to avoid the shortcomings of these direct measurement methods, several indirect measurement techniques have also been proposed.

These indirect measurement techniques can be divided into two types: electrothermal models and electrochemical impedance-based measurement. An example of the former is a study by Pan et al. (2020) where they developed a 3D electrothermal model, with the heat generation given by the electrical model part while the heat propagation given by the thermal model part [14]. Numerous data-driven models are also described in the literature. For instance, the work from Wang et al. (2023) compares different types of neural networks for the temperature estimation of cells [15]. However, these models may be quite complex, which can make them difficult to use on BMSs. The second type of indirect temperature measurements relies on Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) measurements performed on the cell. EIS is a non-invasive technique for the characterization of electrochemical systems. It has been demonstrated that a correlation can be obtained between the temperature of the cell and the various parameters which can be extracted from its EIS spectra [16–18]. For instance, Raijmakers et al. (2014) found a correlation between the intercept frequency and the internal temperature of the cell [19]. Schmidt et al. (2013) showed that the cell impedance’s real part at 10.3 kHz and the cell’s internal temperature can be correlated [20]. A correlation between the imaginary part of the impedance and the temperature of the cell was given by Spinner et al. (2015) [21]. Srinivasan et al. (2011) showed that the cell temperature can also be correlated to the phase of its impedance at frequencies between 40 Hz to 100 Hz. They proposed that this range of frequencies is related to the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer at the anode [22]. This is one of the most interesting options, primarily because it has a good measurement resolution. However, there are two main limitations to the usage of such a technique. Firstly, a method to conveniently determine the frequency required for this measurement has not been provided in any of these papers. This is necessary for its robust implementation. In all the existing literature correlating the phase of the impedance and the temperature, the frequency at which the phase of the impedance utilized is deduced from a large set of tests at different temperatures and SoCs, to find the frequency that is least SoC-dependent and most temperature-dependent. Secondly, BMSs currently used are not EIS-capable.

While addressing the first limitation, determining the required frequency may be possible by post-processing the data obtained from EIS. The EIS spectrum contains information which may not be accessible by only using the Nyquist plot. Post-processing techniques such as the Distribution of Relaxation Times (DRT) can help to analyze the EIS data even better. DRT is the deconvolution of the EIS spectra which enables the distinction of major and minor polarization effects that are normally overlapped in the frequency domain. This analysis may help in the correct selection of the frequency required for the temperature estimation of the cell using EIS [23]. Considering the second limitation, even though currently used BMSs are not EIS-capable, temperature measurement from EIS could be implemented, since it uses the current and voltage measurements readily available to the BMS. Various other useful parameters such as the state-of-charge (SoC) or state-of-health (SoH) can also be inferred from the EIS, making this a promising alternative [24]. Furthermore, the battery industry is moving towards larger capacity cells, rendering the use of parallel cells obsolete. This can enable the economic equipment of each cell with a native, EIS-capable BMS.

In view of the literature outlined above, this paper builds upon a previous work by the same authors wherein a methodology to estimate the internal temperature of a cell using the phase of the cell’s impedance was proposed [23]. The original contribution of that work is the proposition to use the DRT analysis to find the frequency that is related to a phenomenon occurring within the battery, that is SoC-independent but temperature-dependent. This approach does not require a large set of tests and enables the identification of frequency intervals that are invariant of the state of the battery with respect to temperature estimation. This study involves a thorough validation of that methodology in addition to a study of its dependence on the cell’s SoC. For this purpose, a set of tests is performed wherein the cell’s internal temperature is estimated under various load conditions and compared to the temperature obtained from a sensor placed inside the cell.

The remaining part of this paper is structured as follows. Section 2 introduces the theory related to DRT and presents the proposed methodology to estimate the internal temperature. Section 3 details the methodology followed for the insertion of the sensor inside the cell, the characterization of the cell and the validation tests performed. Section 4 presents the results obtained, focusing on the validation tests. Section 5 presents the corollaries from this study. Finally, Section 6 presents the conclusion and future work to be done in this direction.

The EIS is a technique that is widely used within the battery community. EIS measurements are used to build Equivalent Circuit Models (ECM) which describe the electrical behavior of a cell by representing it as combination of electrical components such as resistors, capacitors, and inductors [25–29]. However, the topology of the ECM has to be assumed prior to the parameters fitting, which can lead its overfitting. To circumvent this, DRT can be used.

DRT has been actively used in the electrochemical energy storage community since 2002 when Schichlein et al. used it with the impedance spectra of a solid oxide fuel cell. The DRT function is the deconvolution of the EIS, and the result is a peak-based representation of the cell impedance. The appearance of peaks at different time constants shows that a process which is characterized by that time constant has a relatively significant impact on the cell’s impedance. Hence, the impedance due to each of the processes occurring within the cell can be separated.

The calculation of the DRT function can be performed with several techniques like Fourier transform or maximum entropy. [30,31]. In this study, the Tikhonov regularization strategy has been used as it is one of the most used and effective solutions [32]. The DRT function that is to be plotted, is not known a-priori. Numerous techniques such as the piece-wise linear (PWL) approximation or radial basis functions (RBFs) have been used in the literature to approximate this function. Wan et al. (2015) has shown RBFs to provide better estimation and faster convergence, as compared to PWL [33]. Therefore, in this analysis, the approximation of the DRT function is performed using an RBF.

The DRT analysis can help isolate the contribution of different electrochemical processes to the overall resistance of the cell. In consideration to Li-ion batteries, the time constant range at which the DRT peaks generally appear, and the processes within the cell that they correspond to are shown in Table 1 [34]:

In view of the above, a process that is temperature-dependent and SoH/SoC- independent must be chosen to be correlated to the internal temperature of the cell. Suresh et al. (2002) have shown that the conduction of the Lithium ions within the SEI layer is independent of the SoC of the cell but varies strongly with the temperature [35]. Therefore, the internal temperature of the cell can be indicated by its impedance. Since the cell impedance is a complex number, the real part, imaginary part, modulus, or phase of the impedance of the SEI layer can be used for this purpose. Each of them is described below.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The real part of the impedance depends on the cell’s form factor and capacity. Smaller cells have a higher resistance and are thus conducive to this measurement. However, using the real part for larger cells would be difficult given their low values.
Literature has shown the imaginary part of the impedance of the SEI layer does not vary with the cell’s temperature [35,36].
The modulus of the impedance is also dependent on the cell’s form factor and capacity, and is, therefore, difficult to use.

Srinivasan et al. (2011) showed that the phase of the impedance can be measured with an acceptable signal/noise ratio, regardless of the cell’s size [22]. Hence, this part of the cell’s impedance can be used for its internal temperature estimation.

ce:section.ce:para:
The phase of the cell impedance at a certain frequency is given by the following equation.
(1)
ϕ
f
= arctan
(
Z
f
′ ′
Z
f
′
)
where
ϕ
f
is the phase of the EIS at a frequency f and
Z
f
′ ′
and
Z
f
′
are the imaginary part and real part of the impedance at frequency f , respectively. An increase in the temperature causes a decrease in the resistance of the SEI layer. Since the reactance is only weakly temperature dependent, this causes an increase in the argument of the arctan function, and hence, in the phase. As the arctan is a monotonically increasing function, it will monotonically increase with temperature.

Since, the conduction of Lithium ions within the SEI layer manifests itself as a DRT peak within the time constants 10−3 and 10−2 s (as shown in Table 1), performing the DRT can be used to identify the exact frequency at which the impedance to the flow of Lithium ions within the SEI layer is reflected in the impedance spectra. A correlation can then be established between the temperature of the cell and the phase of its impedance at this frequency.

The experiments for this study are designed to confirm that the temperature estimated from the proposed methodology indeed corresponds to the internal temperature of the cell. For such a confirmation, it is necessary to:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Establish reference measurement: insert a temperature sensor inside the cell being tested, so that the estimated temperature can be compared to the cell’s internal temperature.
Characterization: characterize the cell to obtain the correlation between the phase of its impedance and its temperature enabling the internal temperature estimation using the proposed methodology.
Validation: validate the proposed methodology by applying different electrical and thermal loads on the cell.

ce:section.ce:para: Thus, the experiments to be run can be divided into three parts and are described below.

First, a temperature sensor in placed inside the cell. A 3-wire resistance-temperature detector (RTD) of dimensions 2.3 × 2.0 ×1.3 mm is used for this purpose. It is covered with two layers of polypropylene before insertion into the cell for 3 reasons:

ce:list-item.ce:para: To prevent any reaction of the electrolyte with the sensor. Polypropylene is indeed a coating material used as the separator material in most batteries.
To cover the sharp edges of the sensor and, therefore, minimize electrode damage during sensor insertion.
To prevent short circuits due to the bare-metal parts of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:para:
The insertion of the temperature sensor into the cell is carried out inside a glove box (Argon glove box with
O
2
concentration below 0.2 ppm and water concentration below 1 ppm). The prepared cell is shown in Fig. 1.

GITT and EIS measurements are always performed before and after sensor insertion to confirm that the impact of the sensor on the properties of the cell is negligible. The EIS is performed at 20 ° C and 3.7 V. To bring the cell to 3.7 V, it is charged at constant current of 2.4 A (C/5) up to 3.7 V and then a constant voltage of 3.7 V is applied until the current drops to below 1.2 A (C/10). The EIS is performed within a frequency range of 10 kHz to 10 mHz with an amplitude of 500 mA and measuring 20 points per decade. The GITT procedure, which is used to obtain the OCV vs capacity curve, is performed at 20 ° C by discharging a cell from 4.2 V to 2.7 V using a current of C/10 for 60 min and rest phases of 1.5 h.

A time delay may be introduced on the sensor’s measurements because of its coating with polypropylene. This delay introduced has also been evaluated. To do this, one bare and two coated temperature sensors are attached onto an aluminum plate which is placed in a temperature chamber. The temperature is first set to 30 ° C , followed by a ramp down to 0 ° C where it is held for 45 min. The results of this evaluation are presented later in the results section.

The characterization of the cell is performed after the insertion of the temperature sensor, to obtain the phase-temperature correlation. This correlation is obtained for a cell at 3.7 V by keeping it in the thermostatic chamber at different temperatures and performing EIS measurements after allowing it to reach thermal equilibrium. The exact procedure is as detailed below.

ce:list-item.ce:para: First, the temperature in the thermostatic chamber is set to a predefined value with the cell to be tested placed inside the chamber. The cell is then kept at that temperature for at least 10 h to ensure thermal equilibrium.

This is followed by the EIS procedure which is conducted with an excitation current having a 0 A DC offset and an amplitude of 500 mA (approx. C/20) from 10 kHz to 100 Hz, compatible with the frequency range related to the SEI layer. 20 points per decade (ppd) are measured.
Steps 1 and 2 are repeated for all desired temperatures.

ce:section.ce:para: The result of this characterization is a phase-temperature correlation, which is then used during the validation to estimate the internal temperature of the cell by measuring the phase at a certain frequency.

To ensure that the proposed methodology can be utilized for systems in real-life scenarios, a set of validation tests is designed to replicate the scenarios that a cell will go through. These tests can be categorized into four types:

ce:list-item.ce:para: No load (NL): The test is conducted to validate if the model is applicable to temperatures other than those which were used to build the model. In this test, the cell is first stabilized at 20 ° C . The temperature chamber is then ramped down to 0 ° C and held there for 1.5 h. Thereafter, it is ramped up to 40 ° C and held there for another 1.5 h. Finally, it is brought back to 20 ° C .

On load (OL): This test is performed to verify if the proposed methodology is applicable to a cell heated internally due to a high current. This would mimic a more real-world scenario compared to the NL test. In this test, the temperature chamber is kept at a constant temperature of 20 ° C while the cell is discharged from 4.2 V to 2.7 V at a constant current of 1 C.

Hybrid (HY): This test is designed to superimpose electrical and thermal loads on the cell to allow a complex temperature evolution of the cell. It involves three charging stages separated by two discharging stages, all using a constant current of 12 A (approx. 1 C). A constant voltage stage is not used in this protocol. There is a rest phase of 30 min between each of the charging/discharging stages. Coincidental to the electric load, a thermal load is applied to the cell. The cell begins with a temperature of 20 ° C applied by the temperature chamber. It then moves to 10 ° C , 30 ° C , 0 ° C and 40 ° C spending approximately 1 h 10 mins at each temperature. This timeframe was chosen so that changing temperature can coincide with the charging/discharging of the cell.

Dynamic loading (DL): This test is conducted to validate the temperature estimation methodology on a cell while it is under a dynamic load such as a driving profile. A Worldwide harmonized Light-duty vehicles Test Cycle (WLTC) is used for this purpose, with the EIS being performed every 90 seconds. 1
1 The measurement is performed only once every 90 s due to the number of lines of instructions that can be given to the equipment. However, this can be user defined. The well-known equation used to obtain the power profile for the WLTC from the speed profile is:
(2)
P =
( m ∗ a +
1
2
∗ ρ ∗
C
d
∗ A +
C
r r
∗ m ∗ g )
∗
ν
σ
where P is the power, m is the mass of the vehicle, a is the acceleration, ρ is the air density,
C
d
is the drag coefficient of the vehicle, A is the frontal area of the vehicle, ν is the velocity,
C
r r
is the coefficient of rolling resistance, σ is the battery to wheel efficiency and g is the acceleration due to gravity. The values of all the related parameters are listed in Table 2. The power at each time instance is downsized to match the energy of the cell being tested using the equation:
(3)
P
c e l l , t
=
P
w l t c , t
E
b a t t
∗
E
c e l l
where
P
c e l l , t
is the power at the cell level at time t ,
P
w l t c , t
is the power calculated from the WLTC profile (i.e., from Eq. (2)),
E
b a t t
is the energy of the battery (listed in Table 2) and
E
c e l l
is the energy of the cell. The power profile thus derived along with the speed profile is shown in Fig. 2.
The cell starts at 0 ° C at the beginning of the test. Since the cycle is only 30 min long and does not heat the cell significantly, a temperature ramp is also imposed to get the cell up to 20 ° C .

ce:section.ce:para: All the validation tests, except DL, involve constant EIS measurements approximately every 20 s from 1 kHz to 400 Hz for temperature estimation. For the DL test, the EIS is carried out every 90 s from 1 kHz to 600 Hz (i.e., 1 kHz, 775 Hz and 600 Hz. EIS was performed at only 3 points to minimize the duration of the EIS and thus its impact on the WLTC profile). The EIS is performed at a DC offset corresponding to the current applied to the cell at that time, with an amplitude of 500 mA [23]. During the tests, temperature data from three sensors are measured:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Internal temperature of the cell from the sensor inserted into it.
Surface temperature of the cell from a sensor placed on the casing of the cell in the middle, on the top side (one of the sides with the largest surface area).
Ambient temperature from a sensor placed in the temperature chamber.

ce:section.ce:para:
The advantage of the proposed method is quantified by computing the root mean squared error (RMSE) of the estimated temperature and comparing it to the RMSE of the temperature from the sensor on the surface for each of the three categories of the validation tests. Both RMSEs are computed with respect to the temperature reading obtained from the sensor inserted into the cell. The RMSE is given by:
(4)
R M S
E
m , v
=
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
(
x
m , v
−
X
v
)
2
where, m represents the type of temperature measurement (estimated (EIS) temperature or surface temperature), v represents the type of validation test (no-load, on-load or hybrid), N denotes the total number of measurements, x denotes the value of the temperature measurement corresponding to m and validation test v , and X represents the value of the temperature recorded by the sensor placed inside the cell for the validation test v .

The experiments have been performed at CSEM’s Battery Innovation Hub located in Neuchatel, Switzerland. The equipment used for the tests include a cell cycler from Biologic for the EIS measurements and cycling purposes, a thermostatic chamber for temperature control, and a digital multimeter from Keithley to record the temperature from the temperature sensors. Specifications for each of these are given below.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Cell cycler BioLogic BCS815: battery tester equipped with 32 parallel, 9 V-15 A channels. ±0.01% FSD accuracy on the voltage and ±0.015% FSD accuracy on current, for each available range. EIS capability from 10 kHz to 10 mHz.
Thermostatic chamber (ACS-DM1200): temperature chamber with a volume of 1200 L and −75 ° C /180 ° C temperature range

Digital multimeter (Keithley DMM6500): 15 measurement functions, including capacitance and temperature (RTD, thermistor and thermocouple) with a scanner card (2000-SCAN card) to enable multi-channel measurement providing up to 10 channels of 2-pole measurements or 5 channels of 4-pole measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: A set of cells at 100% SoH are used for these tests. They are 12 Ah lithium-ion pouch cells (Kokam, SLPB065070180) characterized by the Nickel Manganese Cobalt (NMC) on the positive terminal, and graphite on the negative terminal. The nominal capacity is 12 Ah, nominal voltage is 3.7 V and voltage range is 2.7 V to 4.2 V.

The OCV curve and Nyquist plot obtained from the GITT and EIS measurements, respectively, before and after the sensor insertion for one of the cells are shown in Figs. 3 and 4. The procedure of sensor insertion results in a 1.6% reduction in the capacity of the cell and a 4.5% increase in the value ohmic resistance, which can be considered negligible. This increase in the internal resistance can be attributed to the gas trapped inside the cell after the sensor insertion procedure.

As introduced in the methodology, an experiment to evaluate the thermal lag introduced by the coating was conducted which involved the heating/cooling of an aluminum plate with temperature sensors (one bare and two coated) attached to it, inside the temperature chamber, as described in Section 3.1. The result is presented in Fig. 5. The figure shows a zoomed-in view of the complete experiment, focused on the part when the temperature chamber ramps down from 30 ° C to 0 ° C . The y -axis on the left shows the temperature measured by the sensors while the y -axis on the right shows the absolute temperature difference between the bare and coated sensors. The maximum lag observed in such a scenario is around 0.9 ° C . However, the RMSE over the entire ramp period (from 2500 s to 3700 s in Fig. 5) is approximately 0.25 ° C . Therefore, the lag for the coated sensors can be neglected.

The characterization of the cell is performed to obtain the phase-temperature correlation. The characterization is performed after the sensor insertion procedure and not before because this procedure introduces an increase in the impedance as shown in Fig. 4. Hence, a correlation obtained by characterizing the cell before the procedure will be different from that obtained after it and would not work for the validation.

Fig. 6 shows the Nyquist plot and the Bode phase plot for the EIS performed on one cell at different temperatures from 10 kHz to 10 mHz. The Bode phase plot shows that a linear relationship between the phase and the temperature of the cell may be obtained in the high frequency region (>500 Hz). Performing an EIS measurement in this high frequency region has the advantage of low acquisition times as well as better stability during under-load measurements [37].

The frequency at which the phase of the cell impedance is used for the phase-temperature correlation is determined by performing the DRT analysis on the EIS of the cell. The result of the DRT analysis for a cell at 3.7 V and at various temperatures is shown in Fig. 7. In this respect, we can observe that the frequency intervals associated to the transport of Lithium ions within the SEI layer coincide irrespective of the temperature of the cell. The peak related to the SEI layer, highlighted in the figure, occurs around 1000 Hz (i.e., around 10−3s). Hence, the frequency in this range is chosen for the phase-temperature correlation.

The procedure for obtaining the phase-temperature correlation is described in Section 3.2. Table 3 shows the different temperatures at which the three cells were characterized while Fig. 8 shows this correlation for the three cells tested. The data points are fitted with a linear regression of the form
y = m ∗ x + c
, where m is the slope and c is the intercept of the line. This results in an average coefficient of determination of 0.9981. The slopes of the three correlations are close to each other, the standard deviation being below 5% of the mean. This variation can be explained to stem from the procedure of the in-cell sensor insertion, which introduces some variation amongst the cells. Hence, it can be hypothesized that a very similar correlation can be derived for a set of batteries of the same type.

Due to space constraints, Cells 1 and 2 were characterized together first, followed by Cell 3 alone. Cells 1 and 2 were characterized on the whole temperature range from −10 ° C to 50 ° C . But, since it was known that a linear correlation would emerge for which three temperature points would be sufficient, due to time constraints, the characterization of Cell 3 is performed at these 3 temperatures.

The effect of SoC on the correlation between the phase of the cell’s impedance and its temperature is also studied. Fig. 9 shows this correlation for different SoCs of a cell. The high, medium, and low SoC refers to a voltage of 4.2, 3.7 and 3.4 V respectively. The difference in the slopes of the correlations can be considered negligible, the standard deviation being lesser than 2% of the average.

The validation tests were performed to confirm that the internal temperature estimated by the proposed methodology was indeed the internal temperature of the cell. The tests were also performed to evaluate the robustness of the proposed methodology in the face of an electric or thermal load being applied to the cell. As described in Section 3.3 the four types of validation tests performed on the cell are no-load (NL), on load (OL), hybrid (HY) and dynamic loading (DL). The results for each of these tests are divided into three sub-graphs. The upper sub-graph depicts the current and voltage of the cell, as well as the temperature imposed by the thermostatic chamber. The central sub-graph shows the temperature of the cell from the various sources present, described in Section 3.3, along with the estimated temperature using the phase of the cell impedance. The lowest sub-graph depicts the absolute error of the estimated and surface temperature, both with respect to the internal sensor temperature. The equation for the absolute error is:
(5)
E
m , t
=
|
T
m , t
−
T
i n t , t
|
where E is the absolute error, m is the specific method used to measure the cell’s temperature (i.e., either estimation or surface temperature), t is the time,
T
m , t
is the temperature from the respective method (at time t ), and
T
i n t , t
is the internal temperature (still at time t ). For brevity, validation results from one cell have been depicted here.

Fig. 10 shows the results for the NL tests. The NL validation test is performed to verify that the model is applicable outside the set of temperatures used to build the model. The model is built on temperatures −10, 10, 30 and 50 ° C . The NL tests are performed on 0, 20 and 40 ° C . As no electrical load is applied, the voltage of the cell is stable throughout the test, while the current remains around 0 A. The fluctuations in the current are due to the EIS measurements being made continuously. The temperature graph shows that, under external thermal load being applied to the cell, the internal temperature always lags behind the surface temperature which can be explained by the high thermal mass of the cell. The RMSE and absolute maximum error for the surface temperature compared to the internal temperature is 3.0 ° C and 12.2 ° C , respectively. However, the proposed methodology is able to estimate the internal temperature accurately. The RMSE and absolute maximum error of the estimated temperature with respect to the internal temperature is only 0.8 ° C and 2.2 ° C , respectively.

Fig. 11 shows the results for the OL tests. The objective of this test was to estimate the internal temperature of the cell while it was heated internally, using a high current, which would mimic a scenario closer to the real-world as compared to the NL tests. The EIS for temperature estimation is performed under a current DC offset equal to that of the current applied. In Fig. 11, the upper sub-graph shows an initial discharging current of 12 A (approx. 1 C) being applied which coincides with the voltage decreasing from approximately 4.2 V to 2.7 V. The temperature graph shows that, under electrical load, the temperature of the cell increases due to Joule heating during the constant current phase, while it reduces to the ambient temperature once this load is removed. In this case, the surface temperature lags behind the internal temperature of the cell while the estimated temperature leads the internal temperature during the discharge phase.

It may be argued that this difference is due to the inserted temperature sensor itself, rather than the estimation. During the procedure of inserting the sensor into the battery, an air cavity is inadvertently formed around the sensor, which can cause it to lag while recording the actual temperature of the battery. This can be confirmed by the on-load test results. As the temperature of the battery changes, the temperature from the sensor (measuring the electrolyte temperature) should match the estimation (measuring the SEI layer temperature), due to the low thermal mass of the SEI layer. However, the results show that the sensor lags behind the estimation. Even so, having a temperature sensor inside the battery is one of the best ways to closely measure the “true” internal temperature of the battery, in a convenient way. Thus, it can be used for comparison of different battery temperature estimation methods.

The RMSE and absolute maximum error for the surface temperature compared to the internal temperature is 1.7 ° C and 7.2 ° C , respectively. Compared to this, the RMSE and absolute maximum error of the estimated temperature with respect to the internal temperature is 0.9 ° C and 2.7 ° C , respectively, thus reducing the RMSE and absolute error by approximately 47% and 63%, respectively, compared to the surface temperature values.

Fig. 12 shows the results for the HY tests. The objective of this test was to estimate the internal temperature of the cell while it was heated both internally, using a high charging/discharging current, and externally, with the help of the temperature chamber. Following the procedure detailed in Section 3.3 the upper sub-graph shows the three charging stages separated by two discharging stages. In the central sub-graph, data from the internal temperature sensor shows that the cell follows a complex temperature evolution by virtue of the electrical and thermal load being applied simultaneously. For instance, during the first charge stage, the internal temperature first increases due to Joule heating while the temperature inside the chamber is at 20 ° C . But as the temperature of the chamber drops, a drop in the internal temperature of the cell can also be seen, even though the cell is being charged. However, despite this complex evolution of the internal temperature of the cell, the estimated internal temperature from the phase of the cell impedance closely follows the internal temperature sensor, while the surface temperature sensor shows high deviations. The RMSE and absolute maximum error for the surface temperature compared to the internal temperature is 4.5 ° C and 11.8 ° C , respectively. Compared to this, the RMSE and absolute maximum error of the estimated temperature with respect to the internal temperature is only 2.4 ° C and 4.6 ° C , respectively.

Finally, Fig. 13 shows the results for the DL tests. The objective of this test is to estimate the internal temperature of the cell while it is following a WLTC profile. Following the procedure shown in Section 3.3, the upper sub-graph shows the voltage and current of the cell following the WLTC profile. In the central sub-graph, it can be seen that, as anticipated, the cell does not heat up significantly by virtue of the cycling but heats up due to the temperature ramp imposed. This is clear from the fact that the internal temperature of the cell lags behind the surface temperature. Despite the dynamic profile imposed on the cell, the temperature estimation follows the internal one with a good fidelity. The RMSE and absolute error for the estimation compared to the internal temperature is 1.4 ° C and 2.3 ° C , respectively, while these values for the surface temperature are 2.2 ° C and 3.2 ° C , respectively.

The results described above (summarized in Table 4) show that using the proposed methodology, the internal temperature of a cell can be estimated with reasonable accuracy under most conditions. It is hypothesized that there is almost no lag between the temperature and the estimation from the impedance’s phase. This is because the temperature estimation is from the phase of the impedance related to the SEI layer, which is quite thin (30–100 Å [38]). Due to its low thickness, it has a low thermal mass and is easily affected by changes in temperature. Hence, as soon as the temperature of the SEI layer increases, the phase of the impedance changes with negligible delay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the use of the proposed temperature estimation to monitor thermal runaway
ce:section.ce:para: The real-world implementation of this algorithm involves the following two steps:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Characterization: This step includes the inference of the frequency at which the impedance to the flow of Lithium ions within the SEI layer is reflected in the impedance spectra. It also includes the extraction of the temperature vs phase relationship for the targeted cell. It is worth nothing that the characterization for all the cells of a given batch is not necessary since Section 4.2 has shown that the cell-to-cell variance of the temperature vs phase relationship is negligible.

Application: This step includes performing the EIS on each cell of the battery pack at the identified frequency and user defined time interval to infer the cell’s temperature. This step presupposes the presence of a BMS that is EIS-capable.

ce:section.ce:para: Although a validation test until thermal runaway is not performed, the advantage of the proposed methodology with respect to a surface temperature sensor can be estimated. The case of thermal runaway is most closely emulated by the on-load tests, wherein the temperature of the cell increases internally while the external temperature stays relatively constant. Results from this test shows that the surface temperature records a maximum temperature of approximately 24 ° C . To reach this temperature from 20 ° C , the sensor takes nearly 3000 s, while the estimation reaches this temperature within 500 s, which is 6 times faster than the surface temperature sensor.

The characterization performed can also specify the accuracy required on the EIS-capable BMS that could implement this methodology. The slope of the temperature vs phase graph is approximately 2.5 ° C /°. Hence, a maximum error of 0.4°on the phase of the cell’s impedance can be tolerated to obtain a maximum error of 1 ° C on the temperature estimation.

In a previous work by the same authors, a methodology to estimate the internal temperature of a cell using its impedance was described. The methodology involved correlating the phase of the cell’s impedance at a certain frequency to its temperature. The required frequency was determined by using the DRT technique to deconvolute the EIS and find the frequency related to the SEI layer within the cell.

This study builds upon the previous work and performs a comprehensive set of experimental tests to validate the proposed methodology. First, a temperature sensor is placed inside the cell to record its actual internal temperature. Next, a characterization is performed to obtain the phase vs temperature correlation which would enable the temperature estimation. The impact of the cell’s SoC on the temperature correlation is also characterized, Finally, a set of validation tests are performed to heat the cell both internally and externally, while simultaneously using the proposed methodology to estimate the cell’s internal temperature. The RMSE and maximum error of the estimation with respect to the internal sensor has been computed and compared to the error from the surface temperature sensor under various electrical and thermal loads. It is shown that the maximum error for the surface temperature can be 12.2 ° C while the same for the estimation is 2.2 ° C for the same set of loads. It is also shown that the SoC of the cell does not have a significant impact on the model. Hence, this method does not require an SoC model to work alongside it. The estimation can be up to 6 times faster than a temperature sensor on the surface. The model developed also specifies that an accuracy of 0.4°is required on the phase measurement for a maximum error of 1 ° C on the temperature estimation.

The future work involves steps to improve and further validate the robustness and applicability of the methodology. To improve the robustness of the method, it remains to be confirmed if the method holds for aged cells as well. Moreover, the validation was not performed up to thermal runaway temperatures. Hence, to further extend the applicability of the method, this validation is required to verify that the methodology functions despite possible chemical changes within the materials of the cell.

Shubham Sharad Bhoir: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Guillaume Thenaisie: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Claudio Brivio: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Mario Paolone: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Shubham Sharad Bhoir reports financial support was provided by Innosuisse Swiss Innovation Agency. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
